medications.indication_rationale: A preferential COX-2 NSAID provides effective anti-inflammatory analgesia for arboviral polyarthralgia (the source clinician prescribed meloxicam 15 mg daily). In the telehealth context this should be initiated by the reviewing GP after an in-person allergy, renal, GI and anticoagulant screen — not prescribed blind. Take with food.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 15 mg orally once daily with food (as documented in the source note)
medications.duration: short course with GP review — reassess at the in-person appointment; not open-ended
contraindications_in_this_case: Screen for peptic ulcer disease / GI bleeding history before starting
Screen for renal impairment or dehydration (NSAID-related acute kidney injury risk)
Screen for anticoagulant use and NSAID/aspirin allergy or asthma sensitivity
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: NSAID + anticoagulant/antiplatelet — bleeding risk (patient reports none, but confirm before prescribing)
NSAID + renal impairment or dehydration — acute kidney injury risk; ensure hydration

medications.indication_rationale: Reasonable adjunct/background analgesia, though the patient reports it has been ineffective alone. Can be continued alongside an NSAID for additive pain relief. Available OTC.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1 g orally up to four times daily (maximum 4 g/24 h)

medications.indication_rationale: Antibiotics are NOT indicated for a viral polyarthralgia. Empirical antibiotics would be appropriate only if in-person assessment confirmed septic arthritis — prescribing them now is an error of commission and inappropriate antimicrobial use.

Systemic corticosteroids are not first-line for arboviral polyarthralgia and can mask or worsen an undiagnosed joint infection — should not be started before septic arthritis has been excluded in person.

Opioids are not indicated for inflammatory polyarthralgia; NSAIDs plus simple analgesia are the appropriate analgesic strategy. Opioid initiation here would be inappropriate and carries dependence risk.

integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Some evidence for a modest anti-inflammatory adjunct effect in inflammatory arthritis; reasonable as an adjunct if the patient wishes, but it must not replace analgesia, the in-person review, or serology.
Limited and low-quality evidence for pain in this context (the source note listed it as an adjunct). Low harm, but not a substitute for NSAID analgesia or clinical review.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take the anti-inflammatory tablet once daily WITH a meal to protect the stomach and maintain a steady blood level.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Taking an NSAID with food reduces GI irritation and gives consistent anti-inflammatory coverage for the migrating joint pain.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Each day, check whether any single joint has become bright red, hot, or much more swollen than the others, and take your temperature — seek urgent care if either occurs.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Self-monitoring for a converting single septic joint or a rising fever is the key safety behaviour while the arthralgia is managed in the community.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rest the acutely painful joints tonight, then start gentle range-of-motion movements tomorrow as tolerated to prevent secondary stiffness.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Gentle mobilisation once the acute peak settles prevents stiffness and supports functional recovery in post-viral arthralgia.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Adaptive aids (electric jar/can openers, gripping aids) would help a patient with painful wrist synovitis who lives alone, as noted in the source plan.
The patient lives alone and cannot self-care tonight; a check on home supports is reasonable if functional impairment persists.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a joint reaction that can follow a viral infection carried by mosquitoes. It can be very painful and can wax and wane for weeks to months, but it does not destroy the joints and usually settles gradually.
The diagnosis is not certain yet — a blood test (serology) and an in-person joint examination are needed to confirm it and to make sure a joint infection is not being missed.
Take the anti-inflammatory medicine with food. Rest the sore joints tonight and try gentle movements tomorrow to keep them from stiffening up.
Watch for warning signs: if one joint becomes bright red, hot and very swollen, or you get a high fever or shaking chills, you need urgent in-person assessment.

follow_up_plan.interval: In-person GP review within 24-48 hours for joint examination and blood tests; serology results reviewed at about 1 week (as per the source plan)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Joint examination (warmth/erythema/effusion, whether any single joint has become septic), FBC/CRP and BFV/RRV serology results, pain control, functional status and home supports for a patient living alone.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If any single joint becomes bright red, hot, or dramatically swollen, if a high fever (>39 C) or shaking chills develop, or if unable to cope at home — seek urgent same-day / emergency assessment rather than waiting for the planned review.

must_include_items: Recognise a likely post-viral / arboviral polyarthralgia from the migratory pattern, prodrome, faded rash and mosquito exposure
Arrange PROMPT in-person GP review (within 24-48 hours) for joint examination — do not attempt to fully exclude septic arthritis remotely
Request BFV/RRV serology plus FBC and CRP/ESR
Provide analgesia guidance (NSAID such as meloxicam, initiated by the reviewing GP after a contraindication screen; simple analgesia adjunct)
Give explicit septic-arthritis safety-netting: single hot/red/dramatically swollen joint, or high fever/rigors, means urgent in-person / ED assessment
Acknowledge the patient lives alone and address functional support
acceptable_alternatives: Same-day urgent care / GP review instead of 24-48 h if local access makes that easier (acceptable alternative to the routine prompt review)
Continuing paracetamol or recommending an alternative NSAID as the analgesic (acceptable alternative to meloxicam)
errors_of_omission: Failing to screen for or safety-net the septic-arthritis red flags
Not arranging in-person joint examination or serology
Ignoring the social/functional risk of a patient who lives alone and cannot self-care
errors_of_commission: Prescribing antibiotics for a viral polyarthralgia
Over-triaging a haemodynamically stable patient to the emergency department or a 000 ambulance
Prescribing an S4 NSAID without screening for allergy, renal, GI-bleed and anticoagulant contraindications
Starting oral corticosteroids before septic arthritis is excluded in person
Confidently diagnosing 'just a virus' and dismissing the patient with no in-person joint examination or serology
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES recognising the likely benign post-viral cause AND arranging prompt in-person review AND giving septic-arthritis safety-netting. This is an over-triage-resistance case: escalating a stable patient with a negative acute red-flag screen to ED/000 is a scored OVER-TRIAGE defect; dismissing as pure self-care with no exam, serology or safety-net is an UNDER-TRIAGE failure. The correct answer threads between the two at T2.
